Good afternoon Mr. Locke,

We have received multiple emails from you this week which you directed to various entry points into VA.  Understand that every inquiry will ultimately be routed to this office to respond.  In effort to save you time and speed up the response time, we request that you only respond to this chain.  Of course, you may disregard this request but moving forward we will only be responding to this communication chain.

We have reviewed the 46 items that you have identified below, and we determined responded to them previously in prior responses (which we can provide again) or there is no action for us to respond.  If there are ones that are new that we have not responded to feel free to let us know the specific ones and we will review and respond.

As for the inquiry in the Bankruptcy guidance, you have failed to provide the full guidance.  Please review the full guidance below and understand that we must comply with the full guidance.

When a borrower files for bankruptcy protection, servicers are typically prevented from making contact.  Servicers must report the bankruptcy events in VALERI to include the type of bankruptcy (Chapter 7, 11 or 13 filing) and provide updates such as relief filed, relief granted, dismissed, or discharged.  VA will not attempt contact with Veterans who have filed bankruptcy; however, if the borrower reaches out to VA, VA will discuss the Veteran’s options and the loss of entitlement should the loan terminate and VA pays a claim.

At this this time, your loan has not terminated and therefore we cannot “discuss the Veteran’s options and the loss of entitlement should the loan terminate and VA pays a claim.”

As for your request to engage with our attorneys, they do not engage directly with Veterans but through us.  If you have a specific question, we can reach out to them and request them provide us guidance.

We are also in receipt of your inquiry into the VASP program.  VA has not publicly announced this program and does have not a confirmed date for which a new program will be announced.  Implementation of new programs may take many months as the mortgage industry has to adapt policies and technology to any changes made to the VA Home Loan program.

Again, as we have advised you before you can either continue to argue our policies with us or you can begin to work with us and try to resolve your home loan delinquency once the debt is out of the protection of the Bankruptcy courts.

Hello,

I had a couple questions based off what's publicly available from the Housing Policy Counsel and Mortgage Banker’s Association regarding the VASP program.



1. Your July 26, 2023 joint letter states in part “‘As we understand VASP, the decision to modify a borrower's loan rests with the VA,’ … ‘Yet, servicers continue to evaluate their borrowers for all available options. In other words, servicers continue to communicate and engage with their delinquent borrowers until the VA approves the purchase of the loan and accepts transfer of the loan from the servicer.’ This dynamic ‘dislocates the traditional regulatory relationship between the servicer and the borrower.’”



Can’t the current VA refund process be initiated by the VA without the consent of the servicer? That is a technical legal question that I don’t know the answer to, but since it requires a servicing transfer (which is a highly technical process that requires cooperation), and the purchase of the loan from the servicer, the servicer would almost certainly need to consent to it.

 Ultimately, isn’t it solely the decision of the VA as to whether or not a loan will be refunded? Definitely not my understanding as the program currently works.

What’s different between the old refund and VASP? The new program would be mandatory, and instead of a couple dozen of loans a year, it could be tens of thousands. There are a whole lot of other changes, but those are two big ones.



2. Section 1 of VA Circular 26-20-12 explicitly states “The servicer must ensure the borrower has been given every opportunity to pursue all possible loss mitigation options in order to bring their loan current. Failure to do so could impact a future claim payment and could lead to other legal or administrative action(s) against the servicer.” That’s right, but my understanding is that there are currently no loss mitigation options that a servicer must offer.



3. Subsection c of section 5 of VA Circular 26-20-12 states “Servicers should review loan files for all possible loss mitigation options no later than 30 days before the forbearance period is scheduled to end. Servicers should document such reviews in their loan servicing systems. If no loss mitigation options are possible, in cases where the home has equity, servicers must refer the file to the relevant Regional Loan Center for VA’s consideration of a loan refunding.”

VA Circular 26-20-12 was in place from 4/9/2020 until 7/1/2021. In that time, what number of defaulted VA home loans were referred to the VA for possible refunding pursuant to 26-20-12? Haven’t seen a number on this, but based on verbal information from the VA I would guess less than 50.



Lastly, do you guys represent Planet Home Lending? The Housing Policy Council does not represent Planet Home Lending.



Thank you for any consideration.



Michael Locke

Meter’s running.



I have a VA home loan through Planet Home Lending that was in arrears due to a mandatory evacuation in September 2019 from Hurricane Dorian, as well as the COVID-19 pandemic. The VA has a disaster loan mitigation option that could have been applied, but Planet Home Lending did not offer it. The CARES Act was signed in March 2020 and offered protection to homeowners with mortgages backed by the federal government who are facing financial difficulties due to the COVID-19 pandemic, and explicitly states that mortgage assistance must be provided regardless of delinquency status. The VA then issued a circular on April 8, 2020 that stated servicers have to give borrowers the opportunity to pursue all loss mitigation options to bring their loan current. The VA also issued a circular in July 2021 that introduced the COVID- 19 Refund Modification which allows for significant payment reductions and does not require a financial evaluation. Despite this, Planet Home Lending began court proceedings to obtain a foreclosure, and only offered a loan modification with a trial payment period in December 2021, which could have been done at any point during the default period. I rejected the modification offer due to the applicable laws and regulations that favor the veteran, such as 38 U.S.C. § 5103A and Brown v. Gardner.



1. I currently have a VA home loan through Planet Home Lending.

2. In September of 2019 the area the property is located was under a mandatory evacuation due to Hurricane Dorian. The area was declared a federal disaster area in December of 2019.

3. In 10/1/2019 I missed the first home loan payment.

4. In December of 2019 I submitted an incomplete loss mitigation package to Planet Home Lending.

5. On 1/3/2020 I was denied consideration for any loss mitigation options by Planet Home Lending.

6. The VA has a disaster loss mitigation option that allows for a loan modification absent a complete loss mitigation package.

7. The VA disaster loan modification specifies a trial payment plan is used to verify income in lieu of a complete loss mitigation package.

8. Planet Home Lending had the ability to offer a VA Disaster Loan Modification and/or a special forbearance at this point, but does not.

9. In early 2020 the Covid-19 pandemic begins.

10. On 3/27/2020, THE CARES ACT is signed into law.

11. The CARES Act offers protection to homeowners with mortgages backed by the federal government who are facing financial difficulties due to the COVID-19 pandemic, and explicitly states in section 4022(b)(1) that mortgage assistance provided pursuant to the CARES ACT was to be provided "...regardless of delinquency status."

12. The Department of Veterans Affairs makes use of circulars issued via the official VA website to provide current policies and procedures to VA-approved lenders and servicers.

13. The VA explicitly states via their official website that circulars are supplemental to VA home loan regulations.

14. On 4/8/2020, the VA issues “Circular 26-20-12” via the official VA website in response to the CARES ACT.

15. Section 1 of VA Circular 26-20-12 explicitly states “The servicer must ensure the borrower has been given every opportunity to pursue all possible loss mitigation options in order to bring their loan current. Failure to do so could impact a future claim payment and could lead to other legal or administrative action(s) against the servicer.”

16. I was eligible for a disaster loan modification for the entirety of my default period, but at no point was I offered a disaster loan modification.

17. As a result of section 1 of VA Circular 26-20-12, Planet Home Lending was required to offer a disaster loan modification.



If Planet Home Lending continues to maintain that I was ineligible for any loss mitigation options, then the following applies:



18. Section 3 of VA Circular 26-20-12 states “The borrower, not the servicer, is entitled to determine the period of the forbearance...”

19. Planet Home Lending stated the following regarding forbearance applied to my home loan:

“In May 2020, the Loan was flagged as COVID-19 impacted and upon request, was approved for a COVID-19 forbearance plan. The forbearance plan was extended multiple times August 2020 through August 31, 2021. Planet sent the enclosed correspondence to determine if you were still in need of a forbearance and did not receive a response. Therefore, the forbearance expired on August 31, 2021.

On October 5, 2021, upon receipt of an online request from you, you were approved for an extension of the COVID-19 forbearance plan, which was good through November 1, 2021. As an additional courtesy, Planet backdated the forbearance plan to September 1, 2021, waived your late fees and ensured no negative credit reporting was furnished to the three major agencies pursuant to the CARES Act while you were on a forbearance.”

20. Subsection c of section 5 of VA Circular 26-20-12 states “Servicers should review loan files for all possible loss mitigation options no later than 30 days before the forbearance period is scheduled to end. Servicers should document such reviews in their loan servicing systems. If no loss mitigation options are possible, in cases where the home has equity, servicers must refer the file to the relevant Regional Loan Center for VA’s consideration of a loan refunding.”

21. No such review was conducted or documented within the VA Loan Electronic Reporting Interface (VALERI).

22. The VA via 38 CFR § 36.4301 defines “home equity” as “...the difference between the home's reasonable value and the outstanding balance of all liens on the property.”

23. The VA via 38 CFR § 36.4301 defines “reasonable value” as “...that figure which represents the amount a reputable and qualified appraiser, unaffected by personal interest, bias, or prejudice, would recommend to a prospective purchaser as a proper price or cost in the light of prevailing conditions.”

24. Per the applicable appraisal on file with the VA and Planet Home Lending, the “reasonable value” of the home was/is $292,000.

25. At the time of default, the principal balance of the home was $263,329.99.

26. If the first forbearance period expired in August of 2020 and no loss mitigation options were possible, in July of 2020 Planet Home Lending was required to refer the home loan to the applicable VA Regional Loan Center for possible refunding, as there was no way the outstanding balance of the home exceeded the “reasonable value” of $292,000.

27. At no time did Planet Home lending refer the home loan to the applicable VA Regional Loan Center for possible refunding.

28. On 7/1/2021, VA Circular 26-20-12 is rescinded.

29. On 7/23/2021 the VA issues Circular 26-21-13.

30. The purpose of VA Circular 26-21-13 is to provide “...VA’s waterfall of home retention options for helping borrowers affected financially by the COVID-19 pandemic.”

31. VA Circular 26-21-13 also “...announces the COVID-19 Refund Modification, a type of loan modification specific to those borrowers needing payment reductions when exiting COVID-19 forbearance.”

32. While section 5 of VA Circular 26-21-13 identifies eligibility criteria for borrowers to whom the home retention waterfall will apply, no such eligibility criteria is provided for the COVID-19 Refund Modification. It should be noted that Planet Home Lending presently reflects the last payment received on the loan as 1/1/2020.

33. The COVID-19 Refund Modification is a type of loan modification that allows for significant reductions in a borrower's monthly payments. The indebtedness is secured as a junior lien in favor of VA that does not accrue interest or require monthly payments, and only becomes due when the property is sold, the loan is paid off, or the loan is refinanced. VA can purchase indebtedness up to 30 percent of a borrower's unpaid principal balance, and does not require a financial evaluation for every case.

34. On 9/30/21 Planet Home Lending begins court proceedings to obtain a foreclosure.

 35. On 11/4/2021 Isubmits a complete loss mitigation package to Planet Home Lending.

36. On 12/27/2021 Planet Home Lending puts forth an offer for “Standard Modification” (ie Traditional Loan modification) with a trial payment period.

37. Besides in name, the offer is a disaster loan modification.

38. Due to the proceeding facts, I rejected the loan modification as it could have been offered at any time within the default period.

39. The U.S. Congress has put in place a number of laws and practices to favor veterans as it relates to receiving benefits from the VA. This includes 38 U.S.C. § 5103A, which requires the VA to help the claimant develop their claim for benefits. Furthermore, under Brown v. Gardner, 513 U.S. 115, 115 S. Ct. 552, 130 L. Ed.2d 462, any interpretive doubt is to be resolved in favor of the veteran, as seen in the case of Shinseki v. Sanders, 556 U.S. 396, 129 S. Ct. 1696 (2009).

40. Per 38 USC § 5103A, the VA “...shall make reasonable efforts to assist a claimant in obtaining evidence necessary to substantiate the claimant’s claim for a benefit under a law administered by the Secretary.”

41. The VA home loan benefit is authorized by Title 38, Chapter 37 of the United States Code.

42. The VA has a duty to assist my claims for benefits related to my home loan.

43. The VA was and is aware of the issues described herein.

44. The VA failed and refused to act concerning those issues.

45. I was forced to declare bankruptcy due to the VA’s failure and refusal to act.

46. The home is in delinquency due to the VA’s failure and refusal to act.





________________________________

As far as I’m aware, the following hasn’t been explained by the VA:



“From: Michael Locke

Sent: Thursday, June 23, 2022 3:04 PM
To: Veterans Experience Office <Vets-Experience@VA.gov<mailto:Vets-Experience@VA.gov>>; SECVARequest <SECVARequest@va.gov<mailto:SECVARequest@va.gov>>
Cc: Toth, Jennifer, VBACLEV <Jennifer.Toth1@va.gov<mailto:Jennifer.Toth1@va.gov>>; marcus.young@va.gov<mailto:marcus.young@va.gov> <marcus.young@va.gov<mailto:marcus.young@va.gov>>
Subject: Request for Discrepancy Explanation



Can someone please explain the discrepancies between what VA Phoenix Regional Loan Center Loan Administration Officer Marcus Young is saying and what my servicer is saying? I will most likely have further questions based on the answers I receive.



1. Letter from Marcus Young dated 5/20/2022:

“Your loan was reported in default to the VA and due for the 10/1/19 installment. The reason for default was martial difficulties. When we talked to you on August 14, 2020, you confirmed that the reason for default was martial difficulties. According to our notes, you submitted a loss mitigation assistance application in 2019, however the servicer deemed the application incomplete.”



2. Planet Home Lending response on 6/22/2022  to CFPB Complaint:

“Planet’s investigation revealed that you initially submitted an online portal inquiry on December 3, 2019. The submission was partially completed, however, did not indicate whether the disaster hardship was resolved or ongoing. Planet issued both acknowledgement and missing items letters (enclosed) on that same date. To proceed with a Disaster Modification, Planet must verify that the hardship was resolved, the borrower returned to work and with no negative long-term change in the household income occurring. Despite numerous attempts, we did not receive the required information. Therefore, on January 3, 2020, a loss mitigation denial letter (enclosed) was issued.”



3. Letter from Marcus Young dated 5/20/2022:

“Was Mr. Locke ever under the CARES FB?

• Mr. Locke was approved for a Special Forbearance on August 19, 2020. The Special Forbearance was extended on December 10, 2020, and again on April 2, 2021.”



4. I responded to the proceeding as follows:

“Is the above what is reflected in VALERI? If so, does this mean I have CARES ACT COVID-19 forbearance eligibility remaining? If so, why didn’t the VA intervene at the time Planet Home Lending filed for foreclosure and say I had eligibility remaining? Planet Home Lending has provided conflicting information regarding the forbearance dates. Will the VA please clarify with Planet Home Lending the dates where CARES ACT COVID-19 forbearance was applied to the property, as well as request and provide all documentation substantiating as much?”



5. Mr Young responded to the proceeding with the following:

“• Repayment Plan: Approved: 8/19/20, start date: 5/1/20 estimated cure date: 10/1/20

• Special Forbearance Plan: Approved: 8/19/20 estimated cure date: 11/1/20.”



6. Planet Home Lending response on 6/22/2022  to CFPB Complaint:

‘In May 2020, the Loan was flagged as COVID-19 impacted and upon request, was approved for a COVID-19 forbearance plan. The forbearance plan was extended multiple times August 2020 through August 31, 2021. Planet sent the enclosed correspondence to determine if you were still in need of a forbearance and did not receive a response. Therefore, the forbearance expired on August 31, 2021.



On October 5, 2021, upon receipt of an online request from you, you were approved for an extension of the COVID-19 forbearance plan, which was good through November 1, 2021. As an additional courtesy, Planet backdated the forbearance plan to September 1, 2021, waived your late fees and ensured no negative credit reporting was furnished to the three major agencies pursuant to the CARES Act while you were on a forbearance. Therefore, you were provided with a COVID-19 forbearance plan for a total of 18 months, which is the maximum number permitted by the VA for loans with a COVID-19 hardship on or prior to June 1, 2020.’”



________________________________

Subject: [EXTERNAL] Re: Request for Discrepancy Explanation



Per Marcus Young “Understand at this point they have allowed for the authorized amount of time under the COVID FB requirement, they can consider extending forbearance if you qualify for an extended FB.”



If the Planet Home Lending version of events is accepted, Planet Home Lending was required and failed to refer the loan to the VA for refunding in July of 2020.



1. “The servicer must ensure the borrower has been given every opportunity to pursue all possible loss mitigation options in order to bring their loan current. Failure to do so could impact a future claim payment and could lead to other legal or administrative action(s) against the servicer.” See Circular 26-20-12 (1).

2. “Servicers should review loan files for all possible loss mitigation options no later than 30 days before the forbearance period is scheduled to end. Servicers should document such reviews in their loan servicing systems. If no loss mitigation options are possible, in cases where the home has equity, servicers must refer the file to the relevant Regional Loan Center for VA’s consideration of a loan refunding.” See Circular 26-20-12(5)(c).





________________________________

Subject: Re: Amended Servicer Ability to Offer Loss Mitigation Absent Loss Mitigation Package





VA Response:

"The circulars referenced are specific to COVID-19. Your default occurred prior to COVID-19."

MY Response to VA Response:

And?

Whatever measures the VA has put into place through circulars regarding COVID-19 are an extension of and supplemental to the CARES ACT. Congress explicitly stated in section 4022(b)(1) that mortgage assistance provided pursuant to the CARES ACT was to be provided "...regardless of delinquency status."

"Purpose and Background. During the COVID-19 national emergency the Department of Veterans Affairs (VA), through its home loan program, continues to work toward keeping Veterans and stakeholders safe. On March 27, 2020, the President signed into law the Coronavirus Aid, Relief, and Economic Security Act (the CARES Act), Public Law 116-136. The CARES Act protects borrowers with Federally backed mortgage loans who are experiencing financial hardship due to the COVID-19 national emergency. These protections are outlined below. The servicer must ensure the borrower has been given every opportunity to pursue all possible loss mitigation options in order to bring their loan current. Failure to do so could impact a future claim payment and could lead to other legal or administrative action(s) against the servicer. The Cares Act preempts section 3 of Circular 26-20-7, 'Special Relief for those Potentially Impacted by COVID-19.'" See VA Circular 26-20-12(1).



1.       VA Response:

“Answered in previous letters and correspondence.”



My Response to VA Response:

This was a yes or no question. If the VA provided an answer to a similar question asked at an earlier point, that similar question was a yes or no question as well and the VA never provided a yes or no answer.



2.       This was a yes or no question. Please provide a yes or no answer.

communications.subject: FOIA Request Updated VALERI Loan Information 127 Delvalle St. Melbourne Beach, FL 32951
communications.communication:
Hello,

I'm going to need an updated copy of my "Loan information." For reference, here is the previous request you filled. https://www.muckrock.com/foi/united-states-of-america-10/valeri-home-loan-information-128369/#file-1023757

In addition, I'm going to need the expanded information for the following loan events:


Reason for Default Updated filed 5/10/22 and RFD- NationalEmergencyDeclaration

Reason for Default Updated filed 6/23/22 and RFD – MaritalDifficulties

Reason for Default Updated filed 7/1/22 and RFD – NationalEmergencyDeclaration

Reason for Default Updated filed 7/8/22 and RFD – MaritalDifficulties


If it is unclear as to my meaning, each loan event has a downward arrow to the far right, seemingly indicating an ability for more detailed information concerning each particular loan event.

[image/jpeg]

In the event that there is further information on the particular loan events I’m unaware of, I’m requesting that information as well.

This request is being directed to you due to the metadata on the previously provided documents indicated you were the VA employee who handled retrieval.

[image/jpeg]

communications.subject: Re: VA Hotline Case No. 11559463
communications.communication:
Hello,

The questions posed to you on 12/17/2023 were reworded versions of the questions posed to you on 11/16/2023, with a few follow up questions. You refused to answer the majority of questions posed to you on 11/16/2023.

Just for reference regarding the questions:

[image/jpeg]

For clarity’s sake, I am still asking that the questions posed to you on 12/17/2023. I am also asking that you provide yes or no answers to those questions.

I already submitted a loss mitigation package through bankruptcy mortgage mediation in April 2023. The outcome’s not gonna be any different now. A copy of the servicers response to that loss mitigation package is attached.

Also attached are interrogatories and a request to produce submitted by myself in July 2022 in the foreclosure action. The servicer to date has refused to answer these interrogatories and to provide the requested documents. Pursuant to 38 U.S. Code § 5103A(a)(1), I am again asking that VBA Loan Guaranty make a reasonable effort to assist me in obtaining evidence necessary to substantiate a claim for my VA home loan benefit by attempting to obtain answers to the interrogatories and attempting to obtain the requested documents.

Michael Locke

communications.subject: Case No. 11676769
communications.communication:
In your previous e-mail, you stated in part “We have addressed all of these questions through out your delinquency.” Yet that’s completely contradicted by the responses you provided on 11/16/2023. So what gives?
[image/jpeg]

It seems like it would logically follow if these questions had been answered previously, you would have simply said as much, like you did with the first question asked on 11/16/2023.

I take your non-response concerning the duty to assist request to mean VBA Loan Guaranty is refusing the request.

Because they didn’t follow VA Circular 26-20-12, I don’t owe my servicer anything, so it shouldn’t be that hard to figure out.

I was married to a former DCF/CPS investigator/supervisor. You read that and you think whatever,  but what I mean is that child abused investigators for the state have no legal authority and generally have to BS to close out cases. When a report comes in and they go out to investigate, they generally don’t have a warrant or enough to get a warrant, but they still have to close out their cases. It doesn’t matter who it is or what their socioeconomic status is, no one is initially cooperative when a child abuse investigator shows up at their doorstep. So truth be told, they have to be pretty good at BSing. From what I experienced and what the spouses of other child abuse investigators substantiated, it’s hard to turn off the exploitative BS when you come home. They have to do it so much, it becomes second nature. Most child abuse investigators burnout within a year. My ex did it for 9. Beyond that, she’s 99.99th percentile IQ. Way smarter than me, and I imagine you as well. All of this is to say, I’ve been well trained to deal with this situation.


VASP is still going to be a thing, correct? Even so, I’m not counting on that. I’m counting on the circumstances that spurred the NPR investigation aren’t going to change. “A tiger can’t change its stripes,” as the saying goes.

Because my servicer didn’t follow VA Circular 26-20-12, I don’t owe my servicer anything, and because you guys didn’t ensure my servicer followed VA Circular 26-20-12, I don’t owe the VA anything either.

Michael Locke

communications.communication:
I call it “Pulling a blues traveler.”

It doesn't matter what I say
So long as I speak with conviction
That makes you feel I'll convey
Some valid truth or vast reflection
But I've said nothing so far
And I can keep it up for as long as it takes
And it don't matter who you are
If I'm doing my job, then it's your resolve that breaks

There is something amiss
I am being insincere
In fact I don't mean any of this
Still my confession draws you near
To confuse the issue I refer
To familiar heroes from long ago
No matter how much Peter loved her
What made the Pan refuse to grow

Because the hook brings you back
I ain't tellin' you no lie
The hook brings you back
On that you can rely

communications.subject: Re: Generative AI
communications.communication:
1.
[image/jpeg]

2.
“To Whom It May Concern:


Pursuant to the Florida Sunshine Law, I hereby request the following records:

Any and all records created pursuant to the request for a formal advisory opinion submitted on 4/23/2023 to the Florida Bar’s Unlicensed Practice of Law listed below:

I am writing to request a formal advisory opinion on whether pro se use of Casetext's CoCounsel would constitute the unlicensed practice of law.

Casetext's CoCounsel is a legal AI assistant that can perform various tasks, such as document review, legal research memos, deposition preparation, and contract analysis. According to a study by Casetext and Stanford CodeX, GPT-4 scored approximately 297 points on the UBE, which is higher than the passing threshold for all UBE jurisdictions. This score places GPT-4 in the 90th percentile of test-takers.

I am a pro se litigant who is interested in using Casetext's CoCounsel to prepare and file legal documents for my case. I understand that Casetext's CoCounsel does not provide legal advice or representation, and that I am solely responsible for the accuracy and legality of my documents.

However, I am concerned that using Casetext's CoCounsel may be considered the unlicensed practice of law under Rule 10-2.1 of the Rules Regulating The Florida Bar, which defines the practice of law as "the giving of legal advice or legal assistance to another person". I am also aware that Rule 10-9.1 allows the Standing Committee on Unlicensed Practice of Law to issue proposed formal advisory opinions concerning activities which may constitute the unlicensed practice of law.

Therefore, I respectfully request that the Standing Committee on Unlicensed Practice of Law issue a formal advisory opinion on whether pro se use of Casetext's CoCounsel would constitute the unlicensed practice of law in Florida.

Thank you for your time and attention.

Sincerely,

Michael Locke
127 Delvalle St.
Melbourne Beach, FL 32951
Mbl22885@msn.com<mailto:Mbl22885@msn.com>
(321)506-9557<tel:(321)506-9557>

The requested documents will be made available to the general public, and this request is not being made for commercial purposes.

In the event that there are fees, I would be grateful if you would inform me of the total charges in advance of fulfilling my request. I would prefer the request filled electronically, by e-mail attachment if available or CD-ROM if not.

Thank you in advance for your anticipated cooperation in this matter. I look forward to receiving your response to this request within 10 business days.

Sincerely,

Michael Locke”

https://www.muckrock.com/foi/tallahassee-10211/formal-advisory-opinion-request-to-florida-bar-unlicensed-practice-of-law-documents-144915/


3. “Thomson Reuters Corporation Signs Definitive Agreement to Acquire Casetext”


https://www.thomsonreuters.com/en/press-releases/2023/june/thomson-reuters-corporation-signs-definitive-agreement-to-acquire-casetext.html


4. “Over $26 million will be allocated within the coming year to organizations providing legal representation, assistance in court proceedings, and defense in cases related to homelessness for veterans.”

 https://www.disabledveterans.org/va-unveils-major-grants-to-combat-rising-veteran-homelessness/

5. “Proponents of AI tout its potential to increase access to justice, particularly for litigants with limited resources. Our court system has a monopoly on many forms of relief. If you want a discharge in bankruptcy, for example, you must see a federal judge. For those who cannot afford a lawyer, AI can help. It drives new, highly accessible tools that provide answers to basic questions, including where to find templates and court forms, how to fill them out, and where to bring them for presentation to the judge—all without leaving home. These tools have the welcome potential to smooth out anmismatch between available resources and urgent needs in our court system.”

https://www.supremecourt.gov/publicinfo/year-end/2023year-endreport.pdf

6. “Thomson Reuters to Provide Westlaw Precision to U.S. Federal Courts

Agreement brings increased efficiency and accuracy to the Federal Judiciary, dramatically improving research speed and quality”

https://www.thomsonreuters.com/en/press-releases/2024/january/thomson-reuters-to-provide-westlaw-precision-to-us-federal-courts.html

7. “Your expanded role includes two primary tasks: 1) Identify if a VA circular contains a good cause exception to the Administrative Procedure Act notice and comment period, using the provided 'Good Cause Notice and Comment' PDF as a reference. 2) Analyze VA circulars to determine if they contain substantial/legislative rules, based on criteria provided in the Rank and Fugere decisions. Provide clear, accurate analyses, adapting your approach to offer both detailed breakdowns and summary conclusions as required by the user.

https://chat.openai.com/g/g-90yabIedw-rule-identifier

Give me half of whatever you guys project the average agency grant to be and I’ll give you something capable of assisting Veterans that’ll deliver results in short order.

communications.subject: Re: Generative AI
communications.communication:
Hello,


I'm reaching out to propose an enhancement to the legal support services offered to our veterans through VA hospital legal clinics. Since 2019, Thomson Reuters has provided their legal research products to the Federal Judiciary. Recently, on January 3, 2024, their contract was updated to incorporate Westlaw Precision, a tool enriched by AI capabilities, suggesting that its innovative AI-assisted features were a significant factor in the contract's renewal.



The integration of Westlaw Precision has revolutionized legal research by enabling professionals to conduct searches using conversational language and receive comprehensive, AI-generated responses. This has greatly improved the efficiency of legal research, drawing from a robust collection of legal documents for accurate, authoritative results.



Considering the apparent reliability of Precision due to its use by the federal courts, I believe our veterans would greatly benefit from similar access through the VA's legal clinics. This would provide them with unparalleled support in navigating their legal challenges.



I would welcome the opportunity to discuss how we could extend this advanced legal research capability to our veterans.



Warm regards,

Michael Locke

communications.subject: Re: Generative AI
communications.communication:
One could argue that the VA's sole source contract with Westlaw for online legal research services indirectly supports the VA's duty to assist veterans in their benefits claims. Given that the VA recognizes the necessity of such a comprehensive legal research tool for its own use in adjudicating claims and legal analysis, it could be argued that veterans should also have access to similar resources to adequately prepare and support their claims. This access could be seen as part of the VA's obligation to provide all necessary assistance to veterans in substantiating their claims, ensuring they have a fair opportunity to present their cases with the best available legal precedents and interpretations.

https://www.highergov.com/contract-opportunity/da10-vba-westlaw-online-subscription-36c10d22q0005-p-f1709/

files.title: IMG_0743
files.source: Department of Veterans Affairs, Veterans Benefits Administration, United States of America

communications.subject: Re: Generative AI
communications.communication:
The argument that the VA's use of specialized legal research tools like those provided by West Publishing Corporation is essential for resolving benefits issues and that similar resources should be made available to veterans to ensure equity and informed decision-making is compelling. This approach aligns with the VA's duty to assist veterans in preparing and substantiating their claims, as outlined in 38 USC § 5103A. Here are excerpts from relevant case law that support various aspects of this argument:

  1.  Veterans Claims Assistance Act of 2000 (VCAA): The VCAA, codified at 38 U.S.C. § 5103A, mandates the VA to assist veterans in developing their claims for benefits. This assistance includes obtaining records relevant to the claim and providing medical examinations when necessary for decision-making on the claim. The case of David R. Gagne v. Robert A. McDonald illustrates the VA's obligation under the VCAA to make reasonable efforts to assist claimants, highlighting the importance of access to comprehensive resources for claim development??.

  2.  Duty to Assist in Obtaining Evidence: The VA's duty to assist includes making reasonable efforts to obtain relevant records that can help substantiate a veteran's claim. This principle is further elaborated in Golz v. Shinseki, where the court affirmed the VA's duty to assist as found in 38 U.S.C. § 5103A, emphasizing the pro-claimant system and the requirement to develop the veteran's claim fully and sympathetically??.

  3.  Relevance and Reasonable Efforts: The case of Sullivan v. McDonald discusses the VA's duty to assist in obtaining records, noting that this duty extends only to potentially relevant records. This underscores the necessity for access to tools that can help identify and analyze relevant legal and factual information to support claims??.

  4.  Empowering Veterans with Information: The argument for providing veterans with access to legal research tools mirrors the VA's duty to assist, as it equips veterans with the necessary information to actively participate in the adjudication of their claims. Cases like Charles v. Principi highlight the VCAA's impact in eliminating the well-grounded-claim requirement and expanding the VA's duty to assist, which can be seen as supporting the broader principle of empowering claimants with information and resources??.

These cases illustrate the legal framework underpinning the VA's duty to assist veterans in preparing and substantiating their claims and highlight the importance of access to comprehensive information and resources, both for the VA and veterans. Extending access to specialized legal research tools to veterans can be seen as a natural extension of the VA's duty to assist, promoting equity and informed participation in the claims process.

communications.subject: Re: Case No. 11676769
communications.communication:
“The Jenkins’ experience is not an isolated event…. There is a happy ending, of sorts, for the Jenkins's. The VA recently notified them that they will be repaid the equity they had amassed in their Montana home.”

https://web.archive.org/web/20240224183816/https://www.dallasnews.com/opinion/commentary/2024/02/24/veterans-deserve-better-than-bait-and-switch-mortgages/?outputType=amp

Logic would dictate that a home in Montana most likely falls under your purview,  but given my Florida home loan is overseen by a a VA home loan technician in Arizona, logic isn’t something that can reasonably be relied on when dealing with VA Home Loan guaranty. That’s my first thought. My second thought is that whatever they did receive was most likely less than what they were rightfully owed. My third try ought is that in this digitally connected world it shouldn’t be hard to confirm whether they did receive what they were rightfully owed.

From: Michael Locke <mbl22885@msn.com>
Sent: Monday, July 8, 2024 10:34 AM
To: Post, Andrew, VBADENV <andrew.post@va.gov>; Toth, Jennifer, VBACLEV <Jennifer.Toth1@va.gov>; Marcus.Young@va.gov <Marcus.Young@va.gov>
Cc: SECVARequest <SECVARequest@va.gov>; VAVBAWAS/CO/Office of the USB VBA <OfficeoftheUnderSecretaryforBenefits@va.gov>; Toth, Jennifer, VBACLEV <Jennifer.Toth1@va.gov>
Subject: Fw: Notice of Filed Board of Veterans' Appeals Appeal, Outstanding Information Requests, and Resulting Hardships

Dear VBA Loan Guaranty Employee,
I am writing to express my serious concern about a critical misunderstanding in the VA's response dated July 5, 2024, regarding my request for the VA to cover the $27,000 payment obligation stemming from the court-ordered sale of my home.
Your letter states that "VA has no basis for making a $27,000 cash payment to you or your former spouse for your VA-guaranteed loan." However, this statement mischaracterizes the nature and origin of this payment obligation.
The $27,000 is not a separate matter related to my divorce. It is a direct and unavoidable consequence of the court-ordered sale, which itself was based on the inaccurate assertion that my property was at imminent risk of foreclosure. As I've repeatedly explained, this inaccurate assessment resulted from the VA's failure to properly oversee Planet Home Lending and to investigate discrepancies in the reported forbearance timelines. Please note that I have filed a formal appeal with the Board of Veterans' Appeals on June 12, 2024, challenging these and other failures by the VA. A copy of my appeal is attached for your review.
The June 13, 2022 court order (Exhibit 10, p. 5, para. G) explicitly states that the sale proceeds are to be used to satisfy the remaining balance of the equalizing payment owed to my ex-wife due to the pending foreclosure. This payment would not be required if not for the court-ordered sale, which itself would not have occurred had the VA fulfilled its oversight and assistance obligations.
Furthermore, my ex-wife has stated in writing (Exhibit 11) that she is willing to allow me to retain the home if the $27,000 payment is made. This demonstrates that the payment is the only obstacle preventing me from keeping my home.
Additionally, your letter refers to a "special forbearance" being approved in August 2020. To be clear, my requests for forbearance during this period were specifically related to the COVID-19 pandemic. It appears the VA is not properly recognizing or documenting these COVID-19 forbearances, further demonstrating their failure to comply with their own regulations and guidance.
Frankly, I'm deeply disturbed by the VA's attempt to rewrite the history of my loan and to downplay the impact of the COVID-19 pandemic on my situation. Until the VA demonstrates a genuine commitment to accuracy, transparency, and accountability in its oversight of my loan, I cannot engage in any further discussions or attempts to resolve this matter in good faith. I expect the VA to acknowledge and address its failures, not to obfuscate the truth and shift blame.
I request a prompt and thorough review of this matter, in light of my attached BVA appeal, and a response that directly addresses the specific points I have raised.
Sincerely,
Michael Locke

________________________________
From: Michael Locke <mbl22885@msn.com>
Sent: Tuesday, June 25, 2024 2:09 PM
To: mattw@wasingerlawoffice.com <mattw@wasingerlawoffice.com>; LMTC@planethomelending.com <LMTC@planethomelending.com>
Cc: Marcus.Young@va.gov <Marcus.Young@va.gov>; Post, Andrew, VBADENV <andrew.post@va.gov>; Toth, Jennifer, VBACLEV <Jennifer.Toth1@va.gov>; VAVBAWAS/CO/Office of the USB VBA <OfficeoftheUnderSecretaryforBenefits@va.gov>; SECVARequest <SECVARequest@va.gov>; Tanielian, Terri L. EOP/WHO <Terri.L.Tanielian@who.eop.gov>
Subject: Notice of Filed Board of Veterans' Appeals Appeal, Outstanding Information Requests, and Resulting Hardships

Dear Mr. Wassinger,
I am writing to formally notify you that on June 12, 2024, I filed an appeal with the Board of Veterans' Appeals (BVA) regarding decisions made by the Department of Veterans Affairs concerning my VA-guaranteed home loan. This appeal addresses several critical issues, including:

  1.  The VA's oversight of Planet Home Lending's compliance with VA regulations
  2.  The application of COVID-19 forbearance protections
  3.  The VA's duty to assist and apply the benefit of the doubt principle

You are specifically mentioned in this appeal regarding your testimony at the May 25, 2022 hearing in my divorce case, where you asserted an imminent risk of foreclosure on my property. I want to emphasize that this assertion, which my appeal demonstrates was inaccurate, has resulted in significant personal and financial hardships for me. These hardships include, but are not limited to:

  *   A court order to sell my home based on the perceived imminent foreclosure risk

Cc: HVAC Info <hvacinfo@mail.house.gov>
Subject: Fw: VASP and Veterans Housing Stability Act of 2024 and/or VA Home Loan Program Reform Act of 2024

Dear Chairman Van Orden,
I am writing to express my deep concern regarding both your line of questioning during the recent subcommittee hearing on June 12, 2024, concerning the Veterans Housing Stability Act of 2024 and the content of the VA Home Loan Program Reform Act of 2024, which you recently introduced alongside Chairman Bost. While I commend the subcommittee's efforts to address the urgent foreclosure crisis facing veterans, I believe your approach to the VASP program was confusing, potentially misleading, and ultimately counterproductive. Moreover, I have serious reservations about the VA Home Loan Program Reform Act, specifically as it relates to veterans’ due process rights, and your stance on VASP in light of existing statutory authority.
Regarding your questioning about VASP during the hearing:
Your repeated emphasis on the figure of 16 billion dollars as the potential cost of the VASP program, without providing clear context or supporting evidence, created an atmosphere of unwarranted alarm. While this figure is likely derived from estimations of potential taxpayer losses due to foreclosures, it's unclear if it represents a worst-case scenario or a realistic assessment of the program's risks. Conflating this number with the size of a typical mortgage company portfolio, as you did when questioning Ms. Karen Kretzschmar Paul, further muddled the issue. This conflation disregards the unique challenges and complexities associated with managing a program as large and multifaceted as VASP.
Furthermore, your focus on a perceived contradiction between Mr. John Bell’s earlier testimony and Secretary McDonough’s statements regarding potential foreclosures under VASP, while understandable, lacked the necessary follow-through to gain clarity. While you rightly pressed Mr. Bell for a direct answer, his subsequent vague response and your immediate shift to another topic left the discrepancy unresolved and the subcommittee without a definitive understanding of the VA's position on this crucial matter.
Your line of questioning seemed to center on an implied argument that the VASP program is poorly conceived, hastily implemented, and potentially disastrous for both veterans and taxpayers. This argument, while fueled by valid concerns about the VA's competence and transparency, was not effectively articulated or substantiated with concrete evidence during the hearing.
Equally concerning are provisions within the VA Home Loan Program Reform Act that threaten to strip veterans of their due process rights. Declaring certain VA decisions "final and not subject to judicial review" removes a crucial avenue for veterans to challenge potentially unfair or inaccurate decisions. This lack of accountability could lead to situations where veterans are denied relief they rightfully deserve, with no opportunity for independent review.
My concern is further amplified by your apparent disregard for the existing authority granted to the Secretary under 38 U.S.C. § 3720. Subsections (3) and (4) of this statute explicitly empower the Secretary to “pay, or compromise, any claim” related to VA loan guarantees and to “release any right, title, claim, lien or demand, however acquired, including any equity,” These provisions, through their ordinary meaning, grant the Secretary the authority to implement programs like VASP without the need for new legislation. Every Member of the majority has previously emphasized that the best way to respect congressional intent is to adhere to the ordinary understanding of the terms Congress uses. See, e.g., Jam v. International Finance Corp., 586 U.S. 199, 209 (2019); Gross v. FBL Financial Services, Inc., 557 U.S. 167, 175 (2009); Wall v. Kholi, 562 U.S. 545, 551 (2011); BP p.l.c. v. Mayor and City Council of Baltimore, 593 U.S. 230, 237 (2021); Sackett v. EPA, 598 U.S. 651, 723, 727 (2023); Bartenwerfer v. Buckley, 598 U.S. 69, 77, 83 (2023).
If your intention is to fundamentally challenge the existing authority granted to the VA Secretary under 38 U.S.C. § 3720, I implore you to explicitly state as much and provide a clear and reasoned justification for this departure from established legislative intent.
To address the concerns surrounding VASP and the VA Home Loan Program Reform Act, I urge you to prioritize:

  *
Transparency and Evidence: Provide clear and detailed explanations of the VASP program's design, implementation, and potential risks, supporting your assertions with concrete evidence and data.
  *
Clarity and Consistency from the VA: Demand clear and consistent answers from VA officials regarding potential foreclosures under VASP and other loss mitigation programs.
  *
Protection of Due Process: Revise the VA Home Loan Program Reform Act to ensure veterans retain their full right to appeal VA decisions. This includes removing the limitations on judicial review and ensuring veterans have access to the same procedural safeguards afforded to them in other benefit cases.

The foreclosure crisis facing veterans demands a careful, data-driven approach and a commitment to upholding veterans' fundamental rights and honoring Congressional intent. By focusing on transparency, accountability, and due process, we can work together to develop effective and sustainable solutions that protect veterans and ensure the integrity of the VA Home Loan Program.
Michael Locke

From: Michael Locke <mbl22885@msn.com>
Sent: Wednesday, June 12, 2024 1:54 PM
To: HVAC Info <hvacinfo@mail.house.gov>
Subject: Fw: VASP and Veterans Housing Stability Act of 2024 and/or VA Home Loan Program Reform Act of 2024

I am writing to express my profound concern regarding provisions within proposed legislation that would severely restrict veterans' rights to appeal VA decisions concerning home loans. I believe these provisions constitute a clear violation of veterans' constitutional right to due process and will inevitably be challenged in the courts.
Restricting veterans' rights to appeal VA decisions concerning home loans is a clear violation of the Fifth Amendment's Due Process Clause. This clause, adopted in 1791 as part of the Bill of Rights, was intended to protect individuals from arbitrary actions by the federal government. It ensures that the government must follow legal procedures and respect all legal rights owed to a person according to the law. This includes fair notice and an opportunity to be heard before depriving a person of life, liberty, or property. Denying veterans the ability to appeal VA decisions, especially those that could result in the loss of their homes, directly contradicts these fundamental principles.
The VA, like any government agency, is not infallible. Mistakes happen, and there is always a risk of bias or abuse of discretion. Veterans deserve a fair and impartial process for challenging VA decisions that could lead to the loss of their homes.
The proposed restrictions on appeal rights create a dangerous imbalance of power, stripping veterans of essential protections while granting the VA unchecked authority in these critically important matters. This will undoubtedly lead to protracted and costly litigation as veterans are forced to seek redress through the courts to defend their constitutional rights.
I urge the Committee to reconsider these deeply flawed provisions and to prioritize the due process rights of those who have served our nation. Failure to do so will not only harm individual veterans facing foreclosure but will also erode the integrity of the VA Home Loan Guaranty program and undermine public trust in the agency's commitment to veterans' well-being.
Thank you for your attention to this urgent matter. I implore you to uphold the Constitution and defend the rights of our nation's veterans.
From: Michael Locke <mbl22885@msn.com>
Sent: Saturday, June 8, 2024 1:13 PM

Subject: Re: Question Regarding VASP Eligibility and Loan Modifications

Dear Mrs. Tanielian,

I hope this email finds you well. I am writing to bring to your attention the broad authority granted to the Secretary of Veterans Affairs under 38 U.S.C. § 3720(a) and to request that the Secretary exercise this authority to implement a partial claim program and a foreclosure moratorium to provide much-needed relief to veteran homeowners facing hardship.

Under 38 U.S.C. § 3720(a), the Secretary has the power to consent to the modification of the terms of any loan guaranteed, insured, or made under Title 38, including modifications to the rate of interest, time of payment of principal or interest, security, or other provisions. This broad authority has been recently exercised through the issuance of VA Circular 26-24-8, which introduced a 40-year loan modification option for VA-guaranteed loans.

Circular 26-24-8 specifically cites 38 U.S.C. § 3720(a)(2) as the statutory basis for the VA's authority to offer this expanded loan modification option. This provision empowers the Secretary to "consent to the modification, with respect to rate of interest, time of payment of principal or interest or any portion thereof, security or other provisions of any note, contract, mortgage or other instrument securing a loan which has been guaranteed, insured, made or acquired under this chapter."

While the VA has had the ability to offer a 40-year modification for almost three years, as evidenced by Ginnie Mae's announcement in June 2021, the VA only recently allowed for this option through the issuance of Circular 26-24-8 in April 2024.

Given the broad authority granted under 38 U.S.C. § 3720(a), I am respectfully requesting that the Secretary use this power to implement a partial claim program and a foreclosure moratorium to provide critical assistance to veteran homeowners impacted by the current economic crisis. These measures would prevent avoidable foreclosures, keep veterans in their homes, and mitigate losses to the VA loan program.

Please note that I am copying VA leadership on this email, including VA Under Secretary for Benefits Joshua Jacobs and Deputy Director of Policy at the VA Loan Guaranty Service Michelle Corridon, as I intend for this request to be treated as a formal request for benefits subject to appeal to the Board of Veterans' Appeals. If the VA Loan Guaranty Service is going to charge me their day rate, I believe it is only fair that I have the opportunity to fully assert my rights and hold them accountable in kind.

I urge you and the copied VA leadership to take swift action under the Secretary's existing statutory authority to implement these crucial relief measures for veteran homeowners.

Thank you for your time and attention to this critical issue. I appreciate your commitment to advocating for the rights and well-being of our nation's veterans.

Sincerely,

Michael Locke

From: Michael Locke <mbl22885@msn.com>
Sent: Friday, June 7, 2024 12:29 PM
To: Matt Douglas <matt.douglas@housingpolicycouncil.org>
Subject: Question Regarding VASP Eligibility and Loan Modifications

Dear Mr. Douglas,

I hope this email finds you well. I am writing to seek clarification on the Housing Policy Council's understanding of the eligibility criteria for the Veterans Affairs Servicing Purchase (VASP) program, specifically as it relates to veterans who may qualify for other loan modifications.

According to the VA Servicer Handbook M26-4, Chapter 9, Section 9.04(b), servicers are instructed to follow the VA Home Retention Waterfall to determine the most appropriate home retention option for veteran borrowers. The handbook states that if the waterfall review leads to VASP, the servicer should then review the qualifying loan criteria outlined in Section 9.04(b)(1-9).

However, the handbook also notes that "VASP is the final home retention option available in the VA Home Retention Waterfall." It further states that if the loan does not meet the VASP qualifying criteria, the servicer should evaluate and offer any appropriate alternatives to foreclosure.

My understanding of these guidelines is that if a veteran qualifies for another home retention option besides VASP during the waterfall review process, they would not be offered VASP. In other words, qualifying for a different loan modification would likely disqualify a veteran from receiving assistance through VASP.

I am particularly concerned about this issue in light of the recent NPR article titled "The VA tells banks not to foreclose on veterans' homes this year," published on June 7, 2024, by Chris Arnold and Quil Lawrence. The article features the stories of two veterans, Edward O'Conner and Josiah Mena, who seem to be under the impression that they might qualify for VASP despite apparently qualifying for other loan modifications. This apparent discrepancy between the veterans' understanding and the guidelines outlined in the VA Servicer Handbook is troubling and may lead to confusion and false hopes among veteran borrowers.

Can you please confirm if the Housing Policy Council's interpretation of the VASP eligibility requirements aligns with the understanding that qualifying for a loan modification other than VASP would disqualify a veteran from participating in the VASP program? Additionally, I would appreciate any insight you can provide on how the VA and mortgage servicers can work together to ensure that veteran borrowers receive clear, accurate, and consistent information about their eligibility for various home retention options, including VASP.

Thank you for your time and attention to this matter. I look forward to your response and any additional information you can provide to help clarify this important issue for our nation's veterans.

Sincerely,
Michael Locke

Dear Mrs. Tanielian,

I am writing to you today with an urgent plea for President Biden to take decisive action to protect veteran homeowners and safeguard the integrity of the VA Home Loan Guaranty program. The current crisis facing veterans struggling to keep their homes demands immediate attention and a bold response from the White House.

As you know, the VA's decision to end the Partial Claim Payment (PCP) program, coupled with rising interest rates and a lack of adequate loss mitigation options, has created a perfect storm for veteran foreclosures. Thousands of veterans, through no fault of their own, are now at risk of losing their homes, despite having faithfully served our nation.

I understand you are incredibly busy, but I believe this situation is reaching a critical point, demanding immediate intervention. House Veterans Affairs Committee Chairman Bost has publicly stated he is eager to "fix the VA's mess through legislation in the coming weeks," indicating that the "Veterans Housing Stability Act of 2024" is moving forward rapidly. Unfortunately, it appears that the bill is being driven more by the interests of the mortgage industry than the needs and rights of veterans.

Here's why:

  *   Industry's Preferred Solution: The Mortgage Bankers Association (MBA) has stated their preference is for a permanent partial claim program.

  *   VASP Implementation Concerns: While the MBA and Housing Policy Council (HPC) publicly express concerns about the VASP program's implementation, I believe their actions are a calculated tactic to delay and ultimately weaken the program. The supposed lack of details and preparedness conveniently serves their interests by pushing back the implementation of a program they don't truly support.

  *   Congressional Priorities: Chairman Bost's statement highlights the political motivations behind the Act, seemingly focusing on "election year politics" despite claiming the contrary and protecting the VA from "a financial burden of billions of dollars in bailouts for lenders." There is no mention of ensuring veteran protections or holding the VA accountable for its past failures.

Based on these observations, I believe the "Veterans Housing Stability Act" is a deeply flawed piece of legislation that will do more harm than good. It prioritizes the interests of the lending industry over the rights of veterans and will likely result in:

  *   Weakened Loss Mitigation Programs: The VASP program, even if implemented, will be watered down and less effective than it could be due to industry influence and political maneuvering.

  *   Erosion of Due Process Rights: The provision stripping judicial review and redefining "provision of benefits" will leave veterans with fewer options for challenging the VA's actions and holding them accountable for misconduct or negligence.

This push for the Act is a direct response to increased scrutiny of the VA's handling of the Home Loan Guaranty program. As a VA Loan Guaranty employee told me in 2023:

"Nearly every policy in place at the home loan guaranty office is about risk mitigation for the loan, not keeping people in homes. From the 'waterfall' process to the newly proposed ability for the VA to take on non-performing loans as a servicer, most of the positions come from the mindset 'happy lenders will be more likely to offer VA loans.' There's a reason there is a yearly VA lenders conference but almost no outreach to Veterans."

This makes it clear that the VA prioritizes lenders over veteran borrowers, and the "Veterans Housing Stability Act" is an attempt to further cement this imbalance.

Mrs. Tanielian, I urge you to advocate for President Biden to issue an Executive Order directing the VA to take the following actions, all possible under existing legal authorities:

  1.  Institute an Actual Immediate Moratorium on VA Home Loan Foreclosures : This will provide immediate relief and buy time for more effective solutions. The VA has the authority to enact a moratorium under 38 U.S.C. § 3720.

  1.  Reinstate the Partial Claim Payment Program: This program provided a lifeline for veterans, and the VA has the authority to reinstate it using existing funds and resources.

  1.  Direct the VA to Proactively and Meaingfully Engage with Veteran Borrowers: The VA must reach out to veterans facing hardship and provide clear information about available options. This could be accomplished through targeted campaigns, expanded counseling services, and improved communication with veterans service organizations.

By taking these bold steps, President Biden can protect our nation's veterans and demonstrate a commitment to their well-being.

Thank you for your time and consideration. I implore you to advocate for swift action on this critical matter.

Sincerely,

Michael Locke

It is well-documented that military families make up a substantial portion of the volunteer force, with 40% of new recruits having a close family member who has served. This tradition of service is vital to maintaining the strength and readiness of our military.



Weakening veterans' benefits not only undermines the sacrifices made by our service members but also dissuades potential recruits from considering a military career. In a time when the Department of Defense is struggling to meet recruitment quotas , it is crucial to support and honor those who serve, ensuring they and their families receive the benefits they deserve.

The VA's failure to properly oversee Planet Home Lending's compliance with VA regulations, coupled with the servicer's potentially biased actions, directly led to the court-ordered sale of my home. Specifically, the June 13, 2022, court order in my divorce case (Exhibit 15, p. 5, para. G) states that, due to the pending foreclosure, "...the closing proceeds shall be held in escrow until the Court can make a determination as to the correct amount" of the equalizing payment owed to my ex-wife. This sale triggers an immediate payment obligation of approximately $27,000 to my ex-wife, representing the remaining balance on this equalizing payment, which was secured by the property as part of our divorce settlement.



Crucially, the court order to sell my home was based on the assertion that the property was at imminent risk of foreclosure. This assertion, presented through the testimony of Planet Home Lending's attorney, Mr. Wasinger, at the May 25, 2022 hearing (Exhibit [Exhibit Number for Hearing Minutes]), appears to have been inaccurate. VALERI records, as well as my own recollection of when I requested forbearances (and servicer Planet Home Lending's refusal to provide applicable collections records, as well as the refusal of VA employees with direct oversight to intervene and compel Planet Home Lending to provide said collection records), strongly suggests that PHL misrepresented the actual forbearance timeline. The VA failed to apply the benefit of the doubt principle (38 U.S.C. § 5107(b)) in my favor when evaluating this information, despite the clear evidence supporting my position.



According to Planet Home Lending, I was approved for a COVID-19 forbearance plan in May 2020, which was extended multiple times from August 2020 through August 31, 2021 (Exhibit [Exhibit Number for PHL CFPB Response]). However, the VALERI records do not show any special forbearance reported for May 2020 (Exhibit [Exhibit Number for VALERI Records]). Furthermore, I do not recall requesting a forbearance except for May 2020, August 2020, and October of 2021. Despite my requests and the intervention of the VA, Planet Home Lending has refused to provide the applicable collection records that would clarify this discrepancy. Worse yet, the VA being fully aware of this refusal has refused to assist in obtaining these records that would clarify this discrepancy .



VA Circular 26-20-12(3) states that "The borrower, not the servicer, is entitled to determine the period of forbearance..." (Exhibit [Exhibit Number for VA Circular 26-20-12]). However, Planet Home Lending appears to have unilaterally determined the forbearance periods without my input. This, combined with the VA's lack of engagement and their failure to present evidence contradicting the servicer's claims, demonstrates a disregard for their duty to protect my interests and ensure a fair and accurate assessment of the situation.



Furthermore, the VA failed to properly monitor and inform me of my remaining COVID-19 forbearance eligibility, which directly contributed to the court order to sell my home. Despite having 18 months of potential COVID-19-related forbearance available, the VALERI records as of June 2022 (Exhibit [Exhibit Number for VALERI Records]) indicate that I had only utilized 11 months. The VA did not inform me of this remaining eligibility, nor did they intervene to ensure Planet Home Lending considered this option as a means to avoid foreclosure. Had the VA fulfilled its duty to assist and properly tracked my forbearance usage, I could have potentially utilized those remaining 7 months of forbearance to stabilize my financial situation and avoid the court order to sell my home. Their failure to do so represents a direct violation of their obligation to assist me in obtaining the full benefits and protections available under the law.



There is a well-established link between homelessness and increased suicide risk that the VA is fully aware of. Veterans who have experienced homelessness are almost twice as likely as non-Veterans who have experienced homelessness, and 11 times as likely as other Veterans, to report lifetime suicide attempts. Even after controlling for other risk factors, Veterans who have experienced homelessness in their lifetime are nearly four times as likely as other Veterans to have ever attempted suicide. Housing instability is also associated with suicide. In one study, Veterans with past-year housing instability were six times as likely as those who did not experience housing instability to report suicidal ideation. Reducing the likelihood that an individual will experience a suicidal crisis requires addressing factors such as increased financial strain and lack of housing that may contribute to or increase risk for suicide.



The VA has repeatedly stated that preventing Veteran suicide is one of its top priorities. Given this, it follows that the VA should be taking all appropriate actions to prevent unnecessary foreclosures and resulting homelessness among Veterans, knowing the strong association with suicide risk. My situation is a prime example of where the VA failed to intervene and exercise proper oversight despite being notified of the loan servicer's potential violations of VA regulations designed to protect Veterans from foreclosure. This directly led to the court-ordered sale of my home and the triggering of the $27,000 payment to my ex-wife, putting me at greater risk of housing instability and the associated suicide risk.



The VA's inaction has not only resulted in the potential loss of my home but has also created a significant financial burden in the form of the $27,000 payment, exacerbating my stress and anxiety. This financial strain, directly caused by the VA's failures, is a known risk factor for suicide among veterans, further highlighting the VA's moral and ethical obligation to mitigate the harm they have caused and provide financial relief.



While the court has ordered the sale of the property, my ex-wife has expressed her willingness to allow me to pursue other options to keep my home, contingent upon receiving the $27,000 payment. In a message dated May 30, 2024 (Exhibit [Exhibit Number for OFW Message]), she states: "If I am paid the remaining obligation owed to me in the amount of $27,000, I will sign a quit claim deed giving you exclusive ownership of the property at 127 Delvalle St. Melbourne Beach, FL."



I contend that the VA should assume responsibility for this $27,000 payment based on the following:



Direct Consequence of VA Failures: This debt, while stemming from my divorce settlement, is inextricably tied to the property and functions as a secured interest. The court-ordered sale, a direct result of the VA's failure to properly oversee the pending foreclosure proceedings, makes this payment immediately due.



The VA's failure to properly monitor my forbearance usage and inform me of my remaining eligibility directly resulted in the court's perception of an imminent foreclosure risk. This inaccurate assessment, fueled by the VA's inaction, was the primary basis for the court order to sell my home, which, in turn, triggered the immediate need for the $27,000 payment to my ex-wife.



The VA's failure to investigate the discrepancies in the forbearance dates reported by Planet Home Lending and to obtain the servicer's collection records, despite my repeated requests, prevented me from demonstrating that the foreclosure risk was not imminent and that alternative solutions were available. This failure to act directly contributed to the court's decision to order the sale of my home and the resulting financial obligation to my ex-wife.



The VA's failure to fulfill its statutory duty to assist me under 38 U.S.C. § 5103A (Exhibit 3) is a direct cause of this situation. The VA received clear notice of Planet Home Lending's potential violations of VA Circular 26-20-12 (Exhibit 4) through my repeated requests for assistance, documented in emails from August 20, 2023, to March 19, 2024 (Exhibit 1, pp. 4, 5, 10, 53, 57, 65). Despite this, the VA failed to take concrete action to investigate these concerns, obtain relevant documentation from Planet Home Lending, or ensure the servicer was adhering to the circular's mandates. These failures directly led to the imminent risk of foreclosure and the subsequent court order to sell the property, which triggered the immediate need for the $27,000 payment to my ex-wife. Furthermore, the VA's failure to apply the benefit of the doubt principle, as mandated by 38 U.S.C. § 5107(b), directly contributed to the need for this payment. Had they resolved ambiguities in my favor and taken a more proactive approach to assisting me, including properly monitoring my forbearance usage and challenging PHL's claims about imminent foreclosure risk, the court order to sell my home could have been avoided, and this financial obligation would not exist.



Broad Interpretation of "Obligation" under 38 U.S.C. § 3732: The VA has the authority under 38 U.S.C. § 3732(a)(2)(A) to cover "the unpaid balance of the obligation plus accrued interest" upon loan default. I argue that this "obligation" should be interpreted broadly to encompass financial obligations directly stemming from the VA's failure to properly oversee the loan, including the payment owed to my ex-wife. This aligns with the statute's intent to protect veterans from financial losses caused by the mishandling of their VA-guaranteed loans.



For example, this provision could cover legal fees incurred due to my need to defend against the foreclosure action, which was a direct result of the VA's failure to enforce Circular 26-20-12 and hold Planet Home Lending accountable. It could also encompass additional mortgage payments that accrued due to the VA's delay in processing my loss mitigation application and their failure to ensure that Planet Home Lending offered me all eligible options, including the VA Disaster Loan Modification. Furthermore, moving expenses and other costs associated with finding new housing, should I be unable to retain my current home due to the VA's failures, should also be considered under this broad interpretation of "obligation."



Equitable Relief is Warranted: The VA has a moral and ethical obligation to provide equitable relief when its failures contribute to a veteran's financial hardship. By covering this $27,000 payment, the VA can mitigate the harm caused by their inadequate oversight and prevent further financial burden on me, a veteran facing the loss of his home due to failures within the VA Home Loan program.



This payment should be considered a direct consequence of the VA's failure to uphold its responsibilities under 38 U.S.C. § 3732. Covering this cost would not only rectify the financial harm caused by the VA's actions but also demonstrate a commitment to fulfilling their duty to protect veterans from undue hardship stemming from their programs. The VA's responsibility goes beyond a mere technical obligation regarding the loan guarantee - it failed to uphold its fundamental mission and duty to protect the well-being of a veteran under its purview. This should compel the VA to interpret its obligations broadly and provide the equitable relief I am seeking.



The VA's failure to uphold its responsibilities has placed an undue financial and emotional burden on me, jeopardizing my housing stability and well-being. Given the clear connection between the VA's actions and the necessity of this $27,000 payment, I urge the Board to order the VA to cover this cost, demonstrating a commitment to protecting veterans from the harmful consequences of their own administrative failures.

________________________________

Subject: Urgent Follow Up: VFW's Position on Veterans Housing Stability Act of 2024 - Request for Reconsideration



Dear Mr. Murray,

I am writing to follow up on my previous email dated May 10, 2024, expressing my concerns about the "Veterans Housing Stability Act of 2024" (S. 3728) and respectfully requesting that the VFW reconsider its support for the bill in its current form. I am forwarding the original email chain for your reference.

As I attempted to contact you by phone today, I was advised by your receptionist to reiterate my concerns via email. I understand you are incredibly busy, but I believe this issue is of paramount importance to veterans facing foreclosure and warrants your urgent attention.

As detailed in my previous email, I am deeply troubled by Section 3723(f)(3) of the Act, which could be interpreted as stripping veterans of crucial legal protections, including:

  1.  The VA's Duty to Assist (38 U.S.C. § 5103A): This vital protection ensures the VA actively aids veterans in gathering evidence and presenting their claims for benefits. Removing this duty in the context of home loans would leave veterans vulnerable and navigating complex legal proceedings alone.

  1.  The Benefit of the Doubt principle (38 U.S.C. § 5107(b)): This bedrock of veterans' law mandates that the VA give veterans the benefit of the doubt when evidence is balanced or unclear. Eliminating this protection could unfairly disadvantage veterans in disputes with the VA or loan servicers.

  1.  The Right to Appeal to the Board of Veterans' Appeals: This provision could bar veterans from appealing decisions related to the new Partial Claim Program, denying them an avenue for independent review and redress for potentially unjust or erroneous decisions.

My personal experience with the VA Home Loan Guaranty program, outlined in the attached email chain, demonstrates the devastating consequences of a lack of oversight, inconsistent application of regulations, and disregard for veterans' due process rights. This situation is not unique to me, as evidenced by the alarming rate of veteran foreclosures and the VA's ongoing struggles to address this crisis effectively.

Furthermore, I believe the VA already possesses the authority to implement a foreclosure moratorium and a partial claim program without new legislation. The agency could utilize existing authorities under 38 U.S.C. § 3720 to provide immediate relief to veteran borrowers facing hardship.

Therefore, I urge the VFW to reconsider its support for the "Veterans Housing Stability Act of 2024" in its current form. Instead, I believe the VFW should advocate for:

  *   Amendments to the Act: Remove the limitations on judicial review and appeal rights, ensuring veterans retain their full legal protections and access to due process.

  *   VA Action Utilizing Existing Authority: Urge the VA to implement a foreclosure moratorium and a partial claim program using its existing authorities under 38 U.S.C. § 3720 to provide immediate relief to struggling veterans.

This approach would better protect veterans and ensure their access to justice while addressing the urgent foreclosure crisis.

I hope the VFW will prioritize the rights and well-being of our nation's heroes and take a strong stance against any legislation that could erode their hard-earned protections.

Sincerely,

Michael Locke
